observations.recommendation:
We recommended the following to the Municipality:

a. The Accounting Office implement the Perpetual Inventory Method in accounting for inventories as provided under the MNGAS. The purchases should be taken up first in the inventory accounts and every addition or deduction from each item must be duly recorded in the cards and reports that the Accountant and the GSO have to prepare and maintain. More importantly, the Municipal Accountant should discontinue the practice of recording the regular purchases of supplies and materials as an outright expense.
observations.managementAction: Reiterated under AOM No. 2025-12 (2024) dated April 14, 2025.
observations.reason: Perpetual Inventory Method was not yet implemented.

observations.recommendation: c. To provide stock room for the inventory items of the Municipality to protect them from spillage/spoilage and unauthorized use. Also, establish proper control on the receipt and issuance of the items from the suppliers up to the end users.
observations.managementAction: Reiterated under AOM No. 2025-12 (2024) dated April 14, 2025.
observations.reason: The provision for stock room was not yet complied.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Inventory Committee comply with Item 5.11 of COA Circular 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020. Submit the prepared Physical Inventory Plan (PIP) to the Audit Team at least ten (10) calendar days before the scheduled start of inventory taking activities for CY 2024 onwards.
observations.managementAction: Reiterated under AOM No. 2025-12 (2024) dated April 14, 2025.
observations.reason: No PIP has been formulated due to ongoing reorganization of the LGU's Inventory Committee.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the General Services Officer should prepare the reports described in Volume II of the Manual on the New Government Accounting System (MNGAS) for Local Government Units (LGUs) and to Item No. IV (A.5 -A.8) & Item No. VI (2) of COA Circular 2014-002 dated April 15, 2014. Moreover, both are encouraged to visit and apply the new accounting and reporting guidelines prescribed under COA Circular 2024-006 dated March 14, 2024, for the preparation of the Semi-Expendable Property Cards and Semi-Expendable Property Ledger Cards.
observations.managementAction: Reiterated under AOM No. 2025-12 (2024) dated April 14, 2025.
observations.reason: Reports remained unprepared and submitted.

observations.recommendation: b) To provide a justifiable reason for the under assessment and non-collection of permit fees, slaughter fees, and corral fees from slaughterhouse operations. If the reason is not tenable, Audit Team will issue a Notice of Charge totaling P827,440.00.
observations.managementAction: Reiterated under AOM No. 2025-12 (2024) dated April 14, 2025.
observations.reason: Slaughter fees for hogs totaling P200,340.00 remained unremitted.

observations.recommendation: c) To explain the occurrence of variance between the reports provided by the Meat Inspector and the Slaughterhouse Collectors.
observations.managementAction: Reiterated under AOM No. 2025-12 (2024) dated April 14, 2025.
observations.reason: Slaughter fees for hogs totaling P200,340.00 remained unremitted.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our previous recommendation that the Management require the Municipal Accountant to enforce collection and settlement of the long outstanding receivables and cash advances through issuance of demand letters. Otherwise, comply with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2016-005 in requesting for the write-off of the long outstanding receivables.
observations.managementAction: The Management sent demand letters to the Accountable Officers.
observations.reason: Partial settlements were made by some Accountable officials/employees during CY 2024.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our previous recommendation that Management require the Municipal Treasurer to immediately collect the under-collections  amounting to P73,660.13 from the concerned taxpayers due to erroneous computation of imposed interests/penalties on delinquent RPT.
observations.managementAction: The Management confirmed they are already locating the tax payers to enforce the collecting.
observations.reason: Collection is still on-going.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our previous recommendation that Management expedite the titling of the land in the name of the Municipality so that the ownership could be properly secured.
observations.managementAction: The Management is processing the required documents.
observations.reason: Titling of land is not yet fully accomplished due to on-going preparation of the required documents.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our previous recommendation that Management for succeeding projects of the LGU, within five (5) working days from the execution of a contract, the LGU shall submit copies of the same and its related documents pursuant to the above cited circular.
observations.reason: The Management still unable to comply with the prescribed period of submission.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our previous recommendation that Management secure copies of the MOA/TA from the source agencies and furnish the COA Audit Team copies of it for reference and audit purposes.
observations.reason: MOAs/Tas not furnished to the Audit Team.

We recommended that the Management:

a.     Refund the overpayment of salaries totaling P154,933.44;
observations.reason: No refund was made.

observations.recommendation: b.     Adhere strictly to the pertinent provision of Section 107 (f) of the 2017 Omnibus Rules on Appointment and Other Human Resource Actions (ORAOHRA), as amended in the filling up of vacant positions in its plantilla.

observations.recommendation:
1.11 We recommended that the Municipality work together with its Municipal
Accountant to start facilitating the request for write-off/derecognition of the
Due from Local Government Units (LGUs) account totaling P193,943.27. In
addition, the Municipal Accountant shall prepare for the succeeding quarters
the necessary schedules of receivables as prescribed in COA Circular 2023-
008 dated August 17, 2023.

2.10 We reiterated our previous recommendation that the Management require the
Municipal Accountant to enforce the collection and settlement of the long
outstanding receivables and cash advances through the issuance of demand
letters. Otherwise, comply with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2016-005 in
requesting the write-off of the long-outstanding receivables.

3.7 We recommended that the Municipality expedite in facilitating the request for
write-off/derecognition of its Other Receivables account. Likewise, the
Municipal Accountant shall provide complete details such as the exact dates
when the receivables were granted and the updated statuses of the employees,
whether resigned/transferred/dead, in the monitoring report of the Other
Receivables account.

4.12 We recommended the following to the Municipality:
a. To implement the Perpetual Inventory Method in accounting for
inventories as provided under the New Government Accounting System
(NGAS) Manual for Local Government Units (LGUs). The purchases of
the relief goods should be taken up first in the Welfare Goods for
Distribution account and be treated as an expense under the Welfare
Goods Expense account upon distribution to end users.
b. To make necessary adjustments for the above-discussed
misclassifications. Moreover, avoid succeeding misclassification of
accounts when recording a purchase and distribution of the
Municipality’s relief goods.
c. To prepare and maintain Supplies Ledger Cards (Accounting Unit) and
Stock Cards (Property Unit) based on COA Circular 2024-006 dated
March 14, 2024. Likewise, Annexes P, P-1 & P-2 should be prepared based
on the prescribed format provided by COA Circular 2014-002 dated April
15, 2014.

5.11 We reiterated our previous recommendations to the Municipality that:
a. The Accounting Office implements the Perpetual Inventory Method in
accounting for inventories as provided under the MNGAS. The
purchases should be taken up first in the inventory accounts, and every
addition or deduction from each item must be duly recorded in the
cards and reports that the Accountant and the GSO have to prepare
and maintain. More importantly, the Municipal Accountant should
discontinue the practice of recording the regular purchases of supplies
and materials as an outright expense.
b. Provide a stock room for the inventory items of the Municipality to
protect them from spillage/spoilage and unauthorized use. Also,
establish proper control over the receipt and issuance of the items from
the suppliers up to the end users.

6.6 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant make a proper
reclassification of the purchased one (1) unit Toyota Hilux, having an
acquisition cost of P1,600,000.00, from Disaster Response and Rescue
Equipment into a Motor Vehicle under the DRRM Fund for proper
classification.

7.3 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Engineer
reconcile their reports with regard to infrastructure projects. Both must avoid
the occurrence of (a) non-recognition of fully completed projects into its
proper PPE account; (b) projects recorded as completed and classified into
PPE accounts but not yet declared as fully completed by the Municipal
Engineer; (c) inconsistent total costs incurred; and (d) inconsistent dates as to
completion of the projects.

8.14 We reiterated our previous recommendation that Management expedite the
titling of the land in the name of the Municipality so that the ownership could
be properly secured.

9.4 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant correct the overstatement
of the Trust Liability – DRRM account in CY 2024, totaling P739,858.90.
Moreover, she should likewise conduct a regular reconciliation of utilization
reports with the amount reflected in the financial statements pertaining to the
said account to avoid further occurrences of variances in the Municipality’s
records.

10.6 Effect the necessary adjusting entries to reclassify from Due to Officers and
Employers to Leave Benefits Payable account the total amount of
P126,715.17.

10.7 Refrain recording the initial recognition and succeeding payments of
terminal leave benefits under the Accounts Payable and Due to Officers and
Employees account.

11.8 We recommended the Management to observe provisions in Items 3.2.1, 3.2.2
& 3.2.4 of DBM-DOF-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 1 dated
November 4, 2020 by utilizing the 20% Development Fund for projects that
are (a) harmonized with the National and Regional Development Plans
(2023-2028); (b) necessary, appropriate, or incidental to efficient and
effective local governance, and those which are essential to the promotion of
the general welfare of the people; and (c) provided with technical assistance
from National Government Agencies to be more relevant and responsive.

12.4 We recommended that the Management shall ensure that the PPAs of the
LDRRMF for CY 2025 onwards are compliant National Disaster Risk
Reduction and Management Council (NDRRMC)-Department of Budget
and Management (DBM) – Department of Interior and Local Government
(DILG) Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2013-1 dated March 25,
2013.

13.9 We reiterated our previous recommendations that the Municipal Accountant
and the General Services Officer should prepare the reports described in
Volume II of the Manual on the New Government Accounting System
(MNGAS) for Local Government Units (LGUs) and Item No. IV (A.5 -A.8)
& Item No. VI (2) of COA Circular 2014-002 dated April 15, 2014. Moreover,
both are encouraged to visit and apply the new accounting and reporting
guidelines prescribed under COA Circular 2024-006 dated March 14, 2024,
for the preparation of the Semi-Expendable Property Cards and Semi-
Expendable Property Ledger Cards.

14.6 We recommended that the Management coordinate with the Local School
Board (LSB) to include the “Previous Year's Unexpended Balances” when
preparing for the SEF Budget Preparation Form No. 1 (Annex A) to
implement more programs, projects, and activities for the intended
beneficiaries.

15.11 We recommended that the Municipality provide written justification for the
P270,000.00 appropriation and grant of the Extraordinary and
Miscellaneous Expenses in CY 2024. Moreover, such appropriation and
grant should cease in CY 2025 and onwards in compliance with Section
325(h) of R.A. No. 7160 and the Supreme Court decision in the case of Abella
v. Commission on Audit.

a. For stale checks more than six (6) months but not over two (2) years:

Dr: Cash in Bank – LCCA P187,054.97
Cr: Accounts Payable P187,054.97

observations.recommendation:
We likewise recommended the following to the Municipal Treasurer:

a. To notify the payees one (1) month before the checks under her custody will become stale checks.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer already complied with the recommendations.

observations.recommendation: b. If the stale checks which have been unclaimed and the original DV and its supporting documents are still in her custody, she must return the original DV and its supporting documents to the Municipal Accountant and she must likewise present the cancelled checks in the RCI after the last check issued for the period indicated in the report.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer already complied with the recommendations.

observations.recommendation: c. If the checks which became stale in the hands of the payee and which require replacement, she must issue a new check upon submission of the stale check to her. Moreover, a certified copy of the DV from the Auditor must also be requested by her for presentation to the Administrator/Local Chief Executive who shall countersign the check. The Municipal Treasurer should likewise report and attach the cancelled checks to the RCI prepared at the period of cancellation. At the same time, the replacement check should be reported in chronological order in the RCI.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer already complied with the recommendations.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant analyze and identify the cause of the unreconciled variance totaling P5,087,147.87 of the Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds accounts. Also, she is required to make necessary adjustments to arrive at the correct balances of the said reciprocal accounts and reflect it in the financial statements for CY 2023.
observations.managementAction: Adjusting Entries to correct the Due to/ Due from Account were already taken up by the Municipal Accountant.

observations.recommendation: b. To maintain Supplies Ledger Cards (Accounting Unit) and Stock Cards (Property Unit) for the inventories of the supplies and materials. Moreover, refer to the new prescribed format of the said cards in COA Circular 2024-006 dated March 14, 2024.
observations.managementAction: The concerned employees already complied with the recommendation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant make necessary adjusting entries to correct the recorded balance of depreciation expenses and the corresponding accumulated depreciations for the above-listed projects pertaining to CY 2023. With regard to the succeeding projects, she should likewise verify first the correct dates of completion, from the certificates of completion and from the engineering’s records, before computing the corresponding depreciation.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant already complied with the recommendations.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare necessary journal entries to reclassify the recorded completed projects under CIP totaling P3,982,689.94 to its appropriate PPE account. Moreover, the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Engineer should reconcile their records to properly identify the final date of completion and residual value of each project so as to accurately provide a depreciation expense for CY 2023.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant already complied with the recommendations.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the General Services Officer (GSO) completely accomplish the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) in accordance with the instructions provided in Volume 2 of the Manual on the New Government Accounting System (MNGAS) for Local Government Units (LGUs). Likewise, the concerned Officer should prepare the RPCPPE using the prescribed format, Annex 49 from the same Manual.
observations.managementAction: The General Services Officer already complied with the recommendations.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant make necessary adjustments by reclassifying the ten percent retention money totaling P3,349,909.54 recorded in the Accounts Payable account to the Guaranty/Security Deposits Payable account. Moreover, strictly observe the use of proper accounts in the financial transactions for CY 2024 onwards.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant already made the reclassification.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipality provide written explanation for the under remittance of the Pag-IBIG premiums of its job orders for CY 2023. Moreover, the Municipality should instruct the employee in charge of the remittance to immediately pay the P40,000.00 under remitted contributions to the Pag-IBIG agency. Lastly, a separate monitoring report of the premiums withheld and remitted should likewise be maintained by the Municipality for the succeeding months and years.
observations.managementAction: Recommendations were complied by the Management.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare and present for audit the necessary Subsidiary Ledgers of the Municipality’s Due to NGAs account. The Subsidiary Ledgers must be patterned with the prescribed format under Annex 6 of the Manual on the New Government Accounting System (MNGAS) for Local Government Units (LGUs).
observations.managementAction: Subsidiary ledgers were prepared by the Municipal Accountant in accordance with the prescribed format.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the concerned employees of the Municipality provide a justifiable explanation or reasons for the occurrence of duplicated and repeatedly used pictures in liquidating the utilization of the Supplementary Feeding Program. The MSWDO was likewise encouraged to observe the proper and reliable documentation of the said program for the implementation of the remaining and unutilized balance amounting to P1,146,600.00 out of the P2,293,200.00 that was granted by the DSWD Field Office 02.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant comply with Item 4.7 of Local Budget Circular No. 144 dated February 28, 2022, that a separate subsidiary ledger should be maintained for each beneficiary barangay for projects under the Local Government Support Fund – Support to Barangay Development Program (LGSF-SBDP) of the National Task Force to End Local Communist Armed Conflict (NFT-ELCAC).
observations.managementAction: Subsidiary ledgers are now being maintained by the Municipal Accountant.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Finance Committee (LFC), in coordination with other local officials concerned, prepare the quarterly reports on fund utilization and status of program/project implementation of the projects under the Local Government Support Fund – Support to Barangay Development Program (LGSF-SBDP) of the National Task Force to End Local Communist Armed Conflict (NFT-ELCAC) using the prescribed format in Annex C of Local Budget Circular No. 144 dated February 28, 2022.
observations.managementAction: Prescribed format is now being used in the submission of the said quarterly reports.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management instruct the concerned officials and employees to ensure that the succeeding disbursements of their Municipality’s twenty percent (20%) Economic Development Fund (EDF) are completely supported with documentary requirements as stated in COA Circular 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012.
observations.managementAction: Recommendation was complied by the Management.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant provide the necessary disclosures in the Municipality’s succeeding Notes to Financial Statements as described in Item No. VII (2) of COA Circular 2014-002 dated April 15, 2014.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant disclosed the details in the Notes to Financial Statements.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipality send demand letters to the contractors to recover the unrecouped advance payments made totaling P206,785.77. If there is pending valid claim not yet paid to the contractors, the Municipal Accountant should hold the payment of such and apply the unrecouped amount of the advances to contractors. If there’s none, the Municipality is encouraged to file appropriate charges against the contractors and recommend for their blacklisting. In addition, the Management may request the dormant amount of debit entry totaling P134,914.00 to be written-off, subject to the rules and conditions on request for write-off. Lastly, strictly monitor the advances to contractors for the succeeding projects of the Municipality to properly enforce the recoupment based on the progress billings.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our previous recommendation that the Management require the Municipal Accountant to enforce collection and settlement of the long outstanding receivables and cash advances through issuance of demand letters. Otherwise, comply with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2016-005 in requesting for the write-off of the long outstanding receivables.

We reiterated our previous recommendation that Management require the Municipal Treasurer to immediately collect the under-collections  amounting to P73,660.13 from the concerned taxpayers due to erroneous computation of imposed interests/penalties on delinquent RPT.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Property Officer to reconcile the properties recorded in the books and in the RPCPPE. Moreover, the Inventory Committee should locate the unaccounted PPEs totaling P2,603,028.87, verify if not yet disposed or transferred. The Inventory Committee must likewise assess if the properties comprising the variances can be declared as missing and identify the person/s accountable thereof. Further, the reconciliation between the two (2) concerned department heads and the complete inventory taking of PPE items of the Municipality by the Inventory Committee must be regularly conducted for the succeeding years.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant should comply with the Part IV of Item No. 14 of COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated November 23, 2015 that no residual value must be recognized in the computation of the depreciation of the Road Networks. The proper reckoning of the affected accounts to provide the necessary adjustments/corrections in the Municipality’s financial statements must also be done by the Municipal Accountant.
We reiterated our previous recommendation that Management expedite the titling of the land in the name of the Municipality so that the ownership could be properly secured.

We recommended that the Management and its concerned department heads avoid granting cash advances to their Job Order (JO) workers for attending trainings, seminars, workshops and other similar activities.

We recommended that Management revisit the COA Circular 2021-014 dated December 22, 2021 and the Executive Order 170, Series of 2022 of President Rodrigo R. Duterte. The Municipality should fully implement the electronic payment (e-Payment) of all the salaries, bonuses, honoraria, subsidies, allowances, and other similar claims to all its officials and employees. Accordingly, the Municipality is expected to assist all its LGU officials and employees in the enrollment of their respective payroll accounts in the bank for them to withdraw their salaries through an Automated Teller Machine (ATM). Lastly, the Municipality is also encouraged to apply digital disbursements to its other succeeding transactions.

We recommended that Management:
Require the Bids and Awards Committee to submit the basis of 12% VAT in calculating the Abstract of Bid as Calculated submitted by the bidders and written justification for non-compliance with DPWH Department Order No. 197 series of 2016 and Item 32.2 of 2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of RA No. 9184; and
To strictly comply with the provisions of the afore-cited regulations for all bidding of succeeding infrastructure projects.

In view thereof, we recommended that the Management require the contractors of the aforementioned projects to immediately undertake the necessary correction/rectification of the deficiencies indicated in the COA-STAS report, otherwise the corresponding amounts of the deficiencies noted shall be disallowed in audit.  Thereafter, submit to the Audit Team the Statement of Work Accomplished (SWA) on the corrections/rectifications made together with the pictures taken during and after for re-inspection.

We reiterated our previous recommendation that Management provide justification on the approval of the Management and the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) in transacting with the establishment for which no legal documents were obtained.

We reiterated our previous recommendation that Management provide justification on the following:
Inconsistencies observed in the procurement and payment of fuel under Ck. No. 59925985 dated February 2019.
Association of the 2 suppliers involved in Item No. 1 transaction
Proprietor of a Gasoline Station that was frequently favored by the LGU who was at the same time hired by the latter as Skilled Worker.

We reiterated our previous recommendation that Management provide justification on the following:
Inconsistency of information on the procurement of rice during 2019 (Item No. 1) while the distribution happened in November and December 2018 based on documents presented.
Absence of inventory and distribution of re-packed rice.
Duplicated names of recipients in the same activity or attendance sheet with the same and distinct signatures.

We reiterated our previous recommendation that Management for succeeding projects of the LGU, within five (5) working days from the execution of a contract, the LGU shall submit copies of the same and its related documents pursuant to the above cited circular.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended the following to Management:
Require the Municipal Development Council to specify the details of the identified priority projects to be charged against the 20% Development Fund to avoid generic and lump-sum projects. Likewise, stop the practice of implementing generic or lump-sum projects without further authority from the Sanggunian to avoid future audit suspension/disallowance.
Adhere strictly to the provisions of DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-01 on the appropriation, utilization and programming of the 20% of the IRA for development projects. Consider utilizing the fund for projects that yield lasting benefits to the constituents of the Municipality. Likewise, expenditures that are not investment or capital in nature like road re-graveling be charged under the General Fund Proper.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant regularly prepare and certify the monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRM Fund of the Municipality, which should be based on the format provided by COA Circular 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012. Likewise, the MDRRM Officer, through the LDRRMC and the Local Development Council (LDC), should submit to COA on or before the 15th day after the end of each month the said prepared monthly utilization reports upon receipt from the Municipal Accountant.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant should only include in the utilized appropriation portion of the monthly and annual DRRM Utilization Reports the cost of goods, services, vehicles, equipment, and other similar PPAs which were actually rendered and duly delivered to the LGU and were already paid by the latter.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant should revisit the COA Circular 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012, to properly guide her in establishing the correct balance of the Trust Liability-DRRM account of the Municipality’s Statement of Financial Position for CY 2022. She is also enjoined to use the same accounting and auditing rules set by the same Circular for the succeeding financial statement preparations.

We recommended that the Management, together with the MDRRMO and the Budget Officer, would ensure that the PPAs of the LDRRMF for CY 2023 and for the succeeding years are compliant with the DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-73 dated April 17, 2012 and National Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council (NDRRMC)-Department of Budget and Management (DBM) – Department of Interior and Local Government (DILG) Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant follow the prescribed template from Annex D of COA Circular 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012, in presenting the amount and details of the unexpended balance of the LDDRM Fund in the Notes to Financial Statements for the succeeding financial report preparations.

We recommended that Management:
Compel the PESO Manager to submit to the Audit Team the photocopies of TUPAD ID (Annex D of DOLE D.O. 219-2020 format) of beneficiaries for the DOLE-TUPAD Program;
Require the Municipal Accountant to ensure that disbursements of funds are properly supported with complete required supporting documents; and
Henceforth, strictly comply with the terms and conditions stipulated in the Memorandum of Agreement to ensure proper implementation of the program including monitoring of the provision of assistance to the intended beneficiary workers.

We reiterated our previous recommendation that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to request Schedule of Payments and Certification of Outstanding Balance from DA-RFU 02 to verify the accurate amount of all payments made and the following amortizations to be paid for the principal and interest.
We reiterated our previous recommendation that Management secure copies of the MOA/TA from the source agencies and furnish the COA Audit Team copies of it for reference and audit purposes.

We recommended that the Management and its concerned department heads avoid allowing their respective Job Order (JO) workers to claim for travel expenses. The department heads of the offices in which the Job Order worker is assigned must also ensure that, tasks given to JO workers do not require travels that would render them to claim the related expenses.

We recommended that the Management:
Refund the overpayment of salaries totaling P154,933.44; and
Adhere strictly to the pertinent provision of Section 107 (f) of the 2017 Omnibus Rules on Appointment and Other Human Resource Actions (ORAOHRA), as amended in the filling up of vacant positions in its plantilla.

Further, complete names and bona fide signatures of the eligible beneficiaries, authorized claimants on the actual cash distribution and authorized representatives on the performance of the actual work must be specified and affixed, respectively.

We recommended that the MSWDO verify if the two concerned beneficiaries actually received the cash assistance amounting P2,550.00 each. Likewise, proper documentation and safekeeping a copy of relevant documents from preparation until project/activity is completed should be appropriately undertaken. Hence, MSWDO should have produced their own file copy of the reports and other relevant documents so as avoid further requesting of copy from their Regional Office in case an unscheduled evaluation of project/activity is to be made.
We recommended that Management revisit the guidelines on the utilization of 20% development fund and ensure that it is utilized consistent with DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017.

We recommended that Management enforce the direct payment to suppliers through check for all procurement of goods and services pursuant to the above provision.
Further, we recommended that the Management stop the practice of granting cash advances except for travel to officials and employees of the Municipality other than the appointed or designated Disbursing Officer/s pursuant to the provisions provided under COA Circular No. 97-002.

We recommended that Management:
Strictly comply with the provisions of the RA 9184 and its Revised IRR inculcating the principles of a sound government procurement.
Enforce the direct payment to suppliers through check for all procurement of goods and services.
Stop the practice of granting cash advances except for travel to municipal employees of the Municipality other than the appointed or designated Disbursing Officer/s.
Provide clarification on the derivation of ABC for the Construction of Covered Court at Maluno Integrated School-Phase II where the ABC is not approximately the same with the total estimated cost of materials per POW.
Ensure that the materials procured were in accord to the POW prepared as in the case of Construction of Box Culvert at Barangay District 1 procurement of diesel which were not in the POW prepared.

We recommended that Management:
Ensure that the allocation for 20% DF were utilized for projects identified in the annual investment program.
The deficient cash shall be gradually recompensed/restored to the 20% DF account.
Evaluate the relevance of the projects particularly those unimplemented for prior years.  If found to be appropriate to the current time, implementation shall be carried out upon availability of cash back up.

We recommended that Management:
Provide the details of deliverables of the contract or purchase order for equipment rentals.  Ensure that the said documents are acknowledged  by the supplier by signing the “Conforme” portion of the PO/Job Order Contract.
Revisit the process of canvassing of prices and implement sound controls to ensure that prices quoted are actually from bona fide suppliers.
The concerned employees involved in the misrepresentation of canvass sheets identified in the above transaction should be investigated and proper action should be taken.
Provide justification on the approval of the Management and the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) in transacting with the establishment for which no legal documents were obtained.
Lastly, provide justification on the reasonableness of equipment rentals and usage of LGU’s equipment at the same time in the same project for which the usage of LGU’s equipment was not specified in the program of work.

We recommended that the Management through the MSWDO  coordinate with the Barangay Officials in facilitating the request of written confirmation from recipients acknowledging the receipt of the cash assistance or others were authorized to claim on their behalf with attached copy of valid Identification Cards. Complete names and bona fide signatures of the recipients and authorized claimants on the actual cash distribution must be specified and affixed, respectively. Confirmation should likewise include the total amount of cash assistance received separately from PGI and LGU.

We recommended that Management stop the practice of granting cash advances for purposes other than for traveling expenses to its elective officials, and submit explanations on granting cash advances to an elective official and to an employee who was not designated as Special Disbursing Officer for that matter and has no association on the sports event of the schools.

We recommended that Management stop the practice of procuring materials by piecemeal or splitting and ensure that all procurements shall be done through public bidding except those provided in the Procurement Law.  Further, the BAC shall ensure strict compliance to the provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA 9184.
We recommended that Management enforce the direct payment to suppliers through check for all procurement of goods and services pursuant to the above provision.
We recommended that Management and the Accountable Officer provide justification on the fictitious official receipts identified in the liquidation reports submitted.
We recommended that Management provide justification on the procurement of tires which were found to be unreasonable due to its unwarranted replacement.

We recommended that the Management exercise prudence in the utilization of its resources.  Limit the spending of representation expenses within the scope of official meetings/conferences.  Further, revisit the necessity of the entitlement of monthly representation expenses to the Sangguniang Bayan.

Hence, we recommended that the Management revisit the entitlement of monthly representation allowance of concerned municipal officials and employees given the foregoing practice of the LGU in the reimbursement or cash advances for meals and snacks.

We recommended that Management enforce the direct payment to suppliers through check for all procurement of goods and services pursuant to the above provision.  Lastly, submit the lacking official receipt pertaining to reimbursement under Ck. No. 53969922 dated 04/05/2018 totaling P81,970.
We recommended that Management stop the payment of monthly transportation allowance (TA) to a Municipal Official who was assigned with government vehicle pursuant to the above provisions.

We recommended that:
The Management through  the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) who are directly responsible for the procurement process designate specific canvassers in the procurement activities of the Municipality and establish sound controls in order to promote transparency, competitiveness and accountability in procurement process of the Municipality.
The BAC ensure that all the details in the canvass sheets are properly filled out such as name of the canvasser, name of the supplier, supplier’s contact details and address. Moreover, the BAC should verify the genuineness of signatures affixed by the suppliers in the canvass sheets.
The people involved in the misrepresentation of documents (canvass sheets) should be investigated and proper action should be taken.
Discontinue the procurement of goods and services on a reimbursement basis and strictly adhere with the pertinent provisions of R.A. 9184 to protect at all times the interest of the Municipality.
The Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to ensure that payments are made by check directly to the creditors to whom payments.

We recommended that the Management stop the practice of granting honorarium to the Head of Procuring Entity for the completed procurement projects.  Further, the BAC shall submit a copy of the minutes of BAC meeting and notices of award to ascertain the correctness of the said claim for honorarium.

We recommended that Management exercise prudence in the utilization of its resources by enforcing compliance to the provisions on claims for travel as provided in Executive Order No. 298 and COA Circular No. 96-004 dated April 19, 1996.

We recommended that Management ensure that the training/seminar/workshop to be attended by the officials and employees should relate or apply to their present function to properly implement the learnings acquired therein which will contribute to the efficiency and effectiveness in carrying out the operations of the municipality.

We recommended that Management strictly comply with the Decision of the CSC to revert Ms. Cayrel to her former position as Nutrition Officer I (SG 10) at the Rural Health Unit of Benito Soliven, and direct Ms. Cayrel to refund the excess salaries she received from SG 24 to SG 10 and the RATA since March 10, 2018 to present, otherwise, Notice of Disallowance shall be issued wherein the concerned Municipal Officials shall be jointly and severally liable with the claimant.

We recommended that Management direct the HRMO and Municipal Accountant to effect the necessary adjustments in the salaries of the employees concerned.  Ensure that they received the just compensation that is due to them.

We recommended the following to the Management:
Provide the supporting documents for the remaining P1,555 cash assistance.
Provide written explanation on the following:
Reasonableness on the grant of cash assistance at P35 each
Incurrence of names that were doubled but belong to different barangay and whose signature also differs.  Also, there were instances that the manner of receipt to the same name differ, one signature and the other is thumbmark
Revisit the parameters in granting financial assistance in the form of AICS.

We recommended that Management revisit the grant of financial assistance to other LGUs especially to those belonging to higher income classification considering the financial capacity of the LGU.  If warranted, Management may also make proper representation with the other LGUs in the Province of Isabela for the evaluation of the practice on exchange of financial assistance between and among the LGUs in the province.

We recommended the following:
That the Human Resource personnel strictly require all the employees to prepare and submit their monthly DTRs to support their claim for salaries and wages.
That the Accountant only process/accept payrolls with complete supporting documents.
That the Management strictly enforce the “No DTR, No Salary” policy.
For sound internal control, the Management consider the use of biometric system to monitor the time and attendance of the employees.

The Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer take up immediately the necessary adjustment in the books of the Municipality the reconciling items to reflect the factual balance of cash for every given period.  
Establish the nature of the unaccounted variance of P783,144.80 and provide a copy of the reconciliation to this Office.

observations.reason: Reiterated as Observation No. 7 in Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

instructs the OIC-HRMO to issue job description to each personnel hired as job order and  ensure that Monthly Accomplishment Reports are regularly submitted to support their compensation together with their approved daily time records, and

instructs the Municipal Treasurer to ensure that payees sign in the “received” portion of the payroll upon receipt of their compensation.
We recommended that Management adhere to the provisions of Section 4, AO No.17, and procure supplies from local private suppliers only, if not available from DBM-PS.
